perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information . “Confidential Information” shall mean any information, including but not limited to data, techniques, protocols or results, or business, financial, commercial or technical information, disclosed by one Party (each a “Discloser” as applicable) to the other Party (each a “Recipient” as applicable) in connection with the terms of that certain Exclusive License Agreement dated December , 2016 (the “License Agreement”) and identified as confidential at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information . “Confidential Information” shall mean any information, including but not limited to data, techniques, protocols or results, or business, financial, commercial or technical information, disclosed by one Party (each a “Discloser” as applicable) to the other Party (each a “Recipient” as applicable) in connection with the terms of that certain Exclusive License Agreement dated December , 2016 (the “License Agreement”) whether or not identified as confidential at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for information to be 'identified as confidential at the time of disclosure' broadens the scope of 'Confidential Information' beyond what is typically legally protected. It introduces ambiguity as information not explicitly marked confidential could still be considered as such, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act implies that trade secrets must be subject to reasonable measures to protect their secrecy. By removing the 'identified as confidential' requirement, the contract potentially includes information not actively protected, weakening a claim for trade secret protection under the law.
perturbation.justification: The modification broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" by removing the requirement that it be "identified as confidential at the time of disclosure." This undermines the Trade Secrets Act's implication that trade secrets must be subject to reasonable measures to protect their secrecy. Without the 'identified as confidential' requirement, information not actively protected could be considered confidential, weakening a claim for trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 (18 U.S.C. 1 note), which implies a standard of protection for trade secrets, making the removal of the identification requirement a potential contradiction. This falls under 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction', where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” under this Agreement shall not include any information that (i) is or becomes publicly available through no wrongful act of Recipient; (ii) was known by Recipient prior to disclosure by Discloser, as evidenced by tangible records; (iii) becomes known to Recipient after disclosure from a third party having an apparent bona fide right to disclose it without any confidentiality obligation; (iv) is independently developed or discovered by Recipient without use of Discloser’s Confidential Information, as evidenced by tangible records; or (v) is disclosed to another party by Discloser without restriction on further disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” under this Agreement shall not include any information that (i) is or becomes publicly known, regardless of the Recipient's actions; (ii) was known by Recipient prior to disclosure by Discloser; (iii) becomes known to Recipient after disclosure from any third party; (iv) is independently developed or discovered by Recipient; or (v) is disclosed to another party by Discloser without restriction on further disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'wrongful act' qualification from (i) and the 'bona fide right' qualification from (iii) significantly weakens the definition of confidential information. Even if the Recipient actively causes the information to become public, or the third party had no right to disclose, the information is no longer considered confidential under this contract. This conflicts with the standard interpretation of confidentiality and trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires efforts to maintain secrecy for trade secret protection. By removing the 'wrongful act' and 'bona fide right' qualifications, the contract allows for loss of confidentiality even through the Recipient's actions or unauthorized disclosures, contradicting the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy required for trade secret status under UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the protection of confidential information by removing the 'wrongful act' and 'bona fide right' qualifications. This means that even if the recipient causes the information to become public or a third party discloses it without authorization, it would no longer be considered confidential. The law explanation states that UTSA requires efforts to maintain secrecy for trade secret protection. By removing these qualifications, the contract contradicts the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy required for trade secret status under UTSA. Although no legal snippet is provided to confirm that reasonable efforts are required, the Perturbation Explanation states that this contradicts the standard interpretation of confidentiality and trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall have the right to, and agrees that it will, use Discloser’s Confidential Information solely for the performance of its obligations and exercise of its rights under the License Agreement (the “Purpose”), except as may be otherwise specified in a separate definitive written agreement negotiated and executed between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall have the right to, and agrees that it will, use Discloser’s Confidential Information for any purpose whatsoever, except as may be otherwise specified in a separate definitive written agreement negotiated and executed between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'solely for the performance of its obligations and exercise of its rights under the License Agreement' to 'for any purpose whatsoever' completely negates the purpose of confidentiality. This modification allows the Recipient to use the Discloser's confidential information for any reason, including to the detriment of the Discloser, thus creating a contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act criminalizes the misappropriation of trade secrets with the intent to benefit oneself or others, knowing or intending that the offense will injure any owner of the trade secret. By allowing the Recipient to use the Confidential Information for 'any purpose whatsoever,' the contract implicitly permits uses that could be harmful and thus violate the intent, if not the letter, of the Economic Espionage Act concerning the protection of trade secrets.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by allowing the Recipient to use confidential information 'for any purpose whatsoever,' directly contradicts the original intent of protecting trade secrets. 18 USC 1832, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, criminalizes the theft of trade secrets with the intent to benefit someone other than the owner, knowing that it will injure the owner. The modified contract language would permit the Recipient to use the confidential information in ways that could injure the Discloser, thus violating the spirit and potentially the letter of 18 USC 1832. This creates a situation where the contract could be interpreted as permitting actions that are illegal under federal law, thus a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that any breach of this Agreement by it may cause irreparable harm to the other party and that each party is entitled to seek injunctive relief and any other remedy available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that any breach of this Agreement by it will not cause irreparable harm to the other party and that each party is entitled to seek monetary damages only.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that a breach will *not* cause irreparable harm and limiting remedies to monetary damages undermines the ability to seek injunctive relief. Injunctive relief is often necessary to stop the ongoing misuse of confidential information, which can have long-term consequences that monetary damages alone cannot address.
perturbation.law_explanation: Equity law allows for injunctive relief when monetary damages are inadequate to remedy a wrong, especially in cases involving confidential information or trade secrets where ongoing misuse can cause irreparable harm. By precluding the possibility of irreparable harm and limiting remedies to monetary damages, the contract attempts to restrict the remedies available under equity law, contradicting the fundamental principles governing the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: equity | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you equity In law, the term "equity" refers to a particular set of remedies and associated procedures involved with civil law . These equitable doctrines and procedures are distinguished from " legal " ones. While legal remedies typically involve monetary damages , equitable relief typically refers to injunctions , specific performance , or vacatur . A court will usually award equitable remedies when a legal remedy is insufficient or inadequate. For example, courts will generally award equitable relief for a claim which involves a particular or unique piece of real estate , or if the plaintiff requests specific performance. Origin The distinction arose in England where there were separate courts of law and courts of equity . Following this pattern in America, some states created chancery courts which deal only with equitable relief. In other states, the courts of common law were empowered to exercise equity jurisdiction . Today, separate courts of chancery have largely been abolished, as the same court that may fashion a legal remedy has the power to prescribe an equitable one. Expansion of Equitable Relief Courts are sometimes hesitant to impose equitable relief, particularly specific performance. This is because equitable remedies often require the courts to monitor the remedies to make sure that the defendant complies with the court order . However, the Supreme Court has been willing to encourage the use of equitable remedies in certain areas of law; see CIGNA Corp. v. Amara et al., 563 U.S. 421 (2011) . ERISA In CIGNA v. Amara the Supreme Court held that for violations of ERISA, the aggrieved party may be entitled to "obtain other appropriate equitable relief" in addition to legal remedies. In Montanile v. Bd. of Trs. of Nat'l Elevator Indus. Health Benefit Plan, 577 U.S. (2016) , the Supreme Court narrowed the scope of equitable remedies for ERISA violations. In Montanile , the Court held that a party claiming equitable relief under ERISA is limited to the relief typically available in equity. As applied to ERISA, equitable relief will not extend to a plaintiff enforcing an "equitable lien against the defendant's general assets." Further Reading John Norton Pomeroy's A Treatise on Equity Jurisprudence: As Administered in the United States of America : Adapted for All the States and to the Union of Legal and Equitable Remedies Under the Reformed Procedure is generally viewed as an authoritative source on equitable remedies in the United States. [Last reviewed in December of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords EQUITY REMEDIES equitable relief equitable remedies contract law EMPLOYEE RETIREMENT INCOME SECURITY ACT (ERISA) contracts ERISA CIVIL LAW Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal history COMMERCE THE LEGAL PROCESS class actions courts wex articles business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure courts and procedure legal education and practice legal theory Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principles of equity law. The original contract allows for injunctive relief, acknowledging potential irreparable harm. The changed text explicitly states that a breach will *not* cause irreparable harm and limits remedies to monetary damages only. This eliminates the possibility of seeking injunctive relief. The scraped law snippet defines equity as "a particular set of remedies and associated procedures involved with civil law" and states that equitable relief, including injunctions, is awarded "when a legal remedy is insufficient or inadequate." By precluding irreparable harm and limiting remedies to monetary damages, the contract attempts to restrict the remedies available under equity law, contradicting the fundamental principles governing the availability of injunctive relief when monetary damages are insufficient. This constitutes a legal contradiction because it misaligns with established legal principles regarding equitable remedies.
